student_definition: A change in an object's position compared with a reference point.
common_misconceptions: Thinking an object is moving without saying what it is moving compared to.
example_cue: The car changed position compared with the school.

student_definition: The place or object used to tell whether something has changed position.
common_misconceptions: Forgetting that motion depends on what you compare it to.
example_cue: The locker room can be the reference point for a runner.

student_definition: The total length of the path traveled, no matter what direction the object moved.
common_misconceptions: Using the straight-line change instead of adding the whole path.
example_cue: Walk 20 m north and 20 m south: distance is 40 m.

student_definition: How far and in what direction an object ends from where it started.
common_misconceptions: Treating displacement like total distance; leaving off direction.
example_cue: Walk 20 m north and 20 m south: displacement is 0 m.

student_definition: How fast something moves; distance divided by time.
common_misconceptions: Adding direction to speed; confusing speed with velocity.
example_cue: A runner travels 100 m in 20 s, so speed is 5 m/s.

student_definition: The total distance traveled divided by the total time for the whole trip.
common_misconceptions: Using only one short part of a trip instead of the whole trip.
example_cue: Average speed over a trip = total distance ÷ total time.

student_definition: The speed of an object at one specific moment.
common_misconceptions: Thinking it must match the average speed for the whole trip.
example_cue: A speedometer shows instantaneous speed.

student_definition: Speed in a specific direction.
common_misconceptions: Leaving off direction; treating velocity as only speed.
example_cue: 20 m/s north is a velocity.

student_definition: The rate that velocity changes over time. It can happen by speeding up, slowing down, or changing direction.
common_misconceptions: Thinking acceleration only means speeding up.
example_cue: A car going from 10 m/s to 30 m/s in 5 s accelerates.

student_definition: Acceleration in the positive direction, often shown as speeding up in classroom examples.
common_misconceptions: Thinking positive always means good or upward instead of direction/sign.
example_cue: Velocity increases from 2 m/s to 8 m/s.

student_definition: Acceleration in the negative direction; in basic examples this often means slowing down.
common_misconceptions: Forgetting negative values are expected when an object slows down.
example_cue: Velocity decreases from 20 m/s to 0 m/s.

student_definition: A push or pull that can change an object's motion.
common_misconceptions: Thinking a force is needed to keep motion going when no friction exists.
example_cue: Pushing a box applies a force.

student_definition: The overall force after all forces on an object are combined.
common_misconceptions: Adding opposite forces instead of subtracting them; ignoring direction.
example_cue: 7 N right and 3 N left gives 4 N right.

student_definition: Forces that cancel out so the net force is 0 N and motion does not change.
common_misconceptions: Thinking no forces are acting just because the object is not accelerating.
example_cue: A book resting on a table has balanced forces.

student_definition: Forces that do not cancel out, causing a change in motion.
common_misconceptions: Forgetting that direction matters when finding net force.
example_cue: A stronger push to the right than left makes the object accelerate right.

student_definition: An object's tendency to resist a change in motion.
common_misconceptions: Thinking faster objects always have more inertia; inertia mainly depends on mass.
example_cue: A heavy truck is harder to start or stop than a bicycle.

student_definition: A force that opposes motion between surfaces that touch.
common_misconceptions: Thinking friction always stops motion instantly or only happens when an object is moving.
example_cue: Grass slows down a kicked soccer ball.

student_definition: Friction that keeps an object from starting to move.
common_misconceptions: Forgetting that friction can act before an object slides.
example_cue: A box does not move when a small push is applied.

student_definition: Friction between surfaces that are sliding past each other.
common_misconceptions: Confusing sliding friction with rolling friction.
example_cue: A book sliding across a desk slows down.

student_definition: Friction on an object that rolls over a surface.
common_misconceptions: Thinking rolling friction is usually stronger than sliding friction in basic examples.
example_cue: A ball rolling across the floor slows down.

student_definition: A friction-like force from air that opposes an object's motion through the air.
common_misconceptions: Ignoring shape and surface area when comparing falling objects.
example_cue: A flat sheet of paper falls slower than a crumpled paper ball.

student_definition: An attractive force between objects with mass.
common_misconceptions: Thinking heavier objects fall faster when air resistance is ignored.
example_cue: Earth pulls objects downward.

student_definition: All objects with mass attract each other; the strength depends on mass and distance.
common_misconceptions: Thinking gravity only exists on Earth.
example_cue: A satellite stays in orbit because of Earth's gravity.

student_definition: The constant speed reached when air resistance balances the force of gravity on a falling object.
common_misconceptions: Thinking the object stops falling when forces balance; it keeps moving at constant speed.
example_cue: A skydiver eventually falls at constant speed before opening the parachute.

student_definition: The force of gravity on an object's mass.
common_misconceptions: Confusing weight with mass; weight is measured in newtons.
example_cue: A 10 kg object weighs about 98 N on Earth.

student_definition: The amount of matter in an object; in this unit it affects inertia, acceleration, weight, and momentum.
common_misconceptions: Treating mass and weight as the same measurement.
example_cue: Mass is measured in kilograms.

student_definition: A measure of how hard it is to stop a moving object, found by multiplying mass by velocity.
common_misconceptions: Thinking only speed matters; mass matters too.
example_cue: A heavy truck moving fast has a lot of momentum.

student_definition: In a collision, momentum can transfer between objects, but total momentum stays the same if no outside force acts.
common_misconceptions: Thinking momentum disappears in a collision instead of transferring or changing forms with outside effects.
example_cue: A cue ball transfers momentum to an 8 ball.

student_definition: An object at rest stays at rest and an object in motion stays in motion unless acted on by an unbalanced force.
common_misconceptions: Thinking moving objects need a continued force even without friction.
example_cue: A passenger keeps moving forward when a bike stops suddenly.

student_definition: Acceleration depends on net force and mass; force equals mass times acceleration.
common_misconceptions: Forgetting that more mass means less acceleration if force stays the same.
example_cue: A lighter object accelerates more than a heavier object with the same force.

student_definition: For every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force.
common_misconceptions: Thinking action and reaction forces act on the same object; they act on different objects.
example_cue: Air moves backward from a balloon, and the balloon moves forward.
